text: The airway surface liquid (ASL) is a liquid layer on the apical side of the respiratory epithelium, reportedly between 5 to 100 μm in depth (Widdicombe and Widdicombe, 1995), and consists of an inner aqueous periciliary liquid layer (PCL) that spans the length of cilia and the outer gel-like mucus layer. Under physiological conditions, ASL composition and height are regulated via vectorial transport of electrolytes, driven by transepithelial transport and apical secretion of Cl− by (predominantly) CFTR, resulting in passive H2O secretion and, consequently, increased ASL height. Absorption of Na+ at the apical side by ENaC and ENaC’s interaction with the basolateral Na+/K+-ATPase exchanging Na+ for K+ leads to net absorption of Na+, which in turn drives fluid absorption and therefore decreases ASL height (Althaus, 2013; Hollenhorst et al., 2011). Decreased ASL height or ASL dehydration, if not rebalanced, results in cilia collapse and thereby effectively hinders coordinated ciliary beating (Knowles and Boucher, 2002; Matsui et al., 1998; Tarran et al., 2001)
